one_line_takeaway: A practical, near-zero-overhead (~3-4 ms) Python middleware that wraps MCP tool calls with token auth, rate limiting, regex WAF, and logging -- a sensible defense-in-depth baseline whose evaluation is a lightweight proof-of-concept (self-comparison, few hand-crafted attacks) rather than a rigorous security benchmark.
key_finding: MCP Guardian's lightweight controls blocked the tested attack scenarios (destructive commands, excessive request rates, unauthorized/invalid tokens) while adding only modest latency: median latency rose from 25.1 ms (baseline) to 28.9 ms with the Guardian, and 95th-percentile from 32.4 ms to 36.7 ms -- an absolute increase of roughly 3-4 ms, characterized as 10-15% overhead in a controlled local environment. The authors argue this overhead is negligible relative to real-world network hops and LLM inference time, so defense-in-depth middleware is adoptable without meaningfully degrading responsiveness.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MCP Guardian, a middleware/security-first layer that sits between MCP clients and MCP tool servers. Its central mechanism is overriding MCP's invoke_mcp_tool method with a guarded_invoke_tool() that intercepts every tool call at a single control point and applies four controls before forwarding to the underlying server: (1) API-token authentication/authorization (optionally scoped to specific tools or read-only vs admin, with optional short-lived encrypted tokens), (2) per-token rate limiting (default 5 requests/minute, returning HTTP 429), (3) a lightweight regex-based Web Application Firewall that scans request arguments for known-malicious patterns (SQL injection, destructive shell commands like rm -rf, references to sensitive files such as ~/.ssh keys), and (4) request/response logging and optional distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry). It ships as a Python reference implementation drawing on zero-trust and defense-in-depth principles, integrable in ~3 lines of code without restructuring existing MCP servers.
threat_model: Adversary is a malicious or compromised MCP tool server, or malicious user/prompt input flowing to an MCP tool, in an agentic LLM workflow where the model autonomously chains tool calls. The paper (via its literature review, largely citing Invariant Labs, Equixly, Hou et al., and others) catalogs eight attack vectors: tool poisoning (hidden instructions in tool docstrings that exfiltrate SSH keys / mcp.json), tool-name conflicts/typosquatting, shadowing (overwriting a trusted tool's description), installer spoofing, command injection, MCP rug pulls (silent malicious updates), OAuth/token theft and account takeover, and sandbox escape. MCP Guardian's own defensive scope targets a subset of these at runtime: unauthorized/tokenless access, injection-style malicious arguments, and rate/DoS abuse. The paper explicitly concedes it cannot protect against a fully compromised server or malicious code inside the tool itself.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) the WAF is proof-of-concept regex pattern matching, prone to false positives and limited threat coverage vs curated/ML rulesets; (2) centralized local-file logging (mcp_guardian.log) does not scale to large deployments; (3) partial attack coverage -- Guardian cannot stop a compromised server or malicious code inside a tool, requiring complementary sandboxing and code-signing; (4) multi-agent contexts (multiple LLMs sharing one Guardian instance) make per-agent identity tracking and quota enforcement hard. Additional reviewer-noted weaknesses: the evaluation is a self-comparison against an unprotected baseline with no comparison to any competing security framework; security 'efficacy' is demonstrated on a handful of hand-crafted inputs rather than a systematic attack corpus, so no true/false-positive or detection rates are reported; the eight-vector threat taxonomy and the widely-quoted '43% of MCP servers susceptible to injection' figure come from cited third-party sources (Equixly, Invariant Labs, Hou et al.), not from the authors' own measurements; and regex-based blocking of substrings like rm -rf is trivially evadable by obfuscation.

datasets_used: No public/benchmark dataset; authors state the work is a conceptual framework with no newly generated or analyzed datasets
Custom test harness: a weather MCP server exposing a get_forecast tool, used for both security scenario tests and latency load tests
quantitative_results: Median latency: 25.1 ms baseline (no Guardian) vs 28.9 ms with MCP Guardian
95th-percentile latency: 32.4 ms baseline vs 36.7 ms with MCP Guardian
Absolute overhead ~3-4 ms median; characterized as 10-15% overhead in a controlled local environment
Rate limiting: default threshold 5 requests/minute per token; stress test invoked get_forecast 100 times, requests beyond 5 rejected with HTTP 429
43% of MCP server implementations found susceptible to command injection -- ATTRIBUTED to cited Equixly research [7], NOT the authors' own finding
Test hardware: VM with 8-core CPU, Python 3.12
baselines_compared: Baseline (No MCP Guardian): unprotected get_forecast calls -- the only quantitative comparison, a self-comparison against the same system with the security stack disabled
No comparison against any competing/third-party MCP security framework
